food_subgroup: Stir-fried vegetable and mushroom dish with butter and cream sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical shared Zi Char serving of creamy butter abalone mushrooms is approximately 250 g, representing a portion shared among 2–3 diners alongside rice and other dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Butter-fried mushrooms with cream sauce — component estimation based on USDA FoodData Central: Oyster mushrooms (raw), Butter (salted), Heavy cream, and IMR Malaysia MyFCD cooked mushroom entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for creamy butter abalone mushroom dish in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 60% abalone/oyster mushrooms (USDA FDC #169401 raw oyster mushroom, adjusted for cooking), 15% butter (USDA FDC #173430), 15% heavy cream (USDA FDC #170859), 10% minor ingredients (garlic, egg yolk, curry leaves, seasoning). Cooking losses applied per standard FAO/INFOODS retention factors. Values represent best-estimate per 100 g of finished dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mushrooms and low-carbohydrate cooked vegetable dishes — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for creamy butter abalone mushroom dish. GI estimated at 15 based on: (1) oyster/abalone mushrooms have very low digestible carbohydrate content and a GI of approximately 10–20 per University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for cooked mushrooms; (2) the butter and cream components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates; (3) overall dish carbohydrate is 5.4 g/100 g with significant fat content further blunting glycemic response. GL per 250 g serving = (15 × 13.5 g available carb) / 100 = 2.0. Classified as low GI (≤55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a 250 g serving contributes ~775 mg sodium, representing approximately 34% of the 2300 mg daily limit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced salt and butter when ordering, as the sodium from seasoning and butter can contribute substantially to daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low net carbohydrate content (5.4 g/100 g); glycemic load per serving is approximately 2.0
type2_diabetes.guidance: Creamy butter abalone mushrooms have a very low glycemic index and minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose, making them a suitable side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the high saturated fat content from butter and cream warrants attention for overall dietary fat management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (~7.9 g/100 g) from butter and cream, with cholesterol approximately 52 mg/100 g; a 250 g serving provides ~19.8 g saturated fat, exceeding the recommended daily limit of 20 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Due to the high saturated fat load from butter and heavy cream, individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in small portions (≤100 g) and infrequently, as regular consumption may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Creamy Butter Abalone Mushrooms is a popular Zi Char (Malaysian-Singaporean wok-cooked) dish featuring abalone mushrooms (Pleurotus abalonus) stir-fried in a rich, aromatic sauce made from butter, heavy cream or evaporated milk, egg yolk, garlic, curry leaves, and chilli padi. The dish is characterised by its luxuriously creamy, golden-yellow sauce that coats the tender, slightly chewy mushroom slices, delivering a savoury, mildly spicy, and subtly sweet flavour profile. Abalone mushrooms are prized for their firm yet silky texture and mild umami taste, which absorbs the butter-cream sauce effectively during wok cooking. The dish is commonly served as a shared side dish in Malaysian and Singaporean Zi Char restaurants alongside steamed white rice. Nutritionally, it is calorie-dense due to its butter and cream base (~148 kcal/100 g), with moderate protein (3.2 g/100 g) from mushrooms and egg, high fat (12.8 g/100 g, predominantly saturated), and low carbohydrate (5.4 g/100 g). Its glycemic index is very low (~15), making it suitable for blood sugar management, but its saturated fat and sodium content require moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease or hypertension. The dish is vegetarian, halal-friendly, and gluten-free. It is sometimes called 'butter mushrooms' or 'creamy mushroom stir-fry' in casual settings.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central entries for oyster mushrooms, butter, and heavy cream, with standard cooking retention factors applied. The food name in the input ('Creamy Buttera Balonemu Shrooms') appears to be a phonetically distorted or OCR-corrupted rendering of 'Creamy Butter Abalone Mushrooms', which is the canonical English name used. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked mushroom dishes. Confidence is medium due to the estimation basis.
notes: 1. The input food name 'Creamy Buttera Balonemu Shrooms' is interpreted as a corrupted or informal rendering of 'Creamy Butter Abalone Mushrooms'; the canonical name has been corrected accordingly. 2. Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis (USDA FDC oyster mushrooms #169401, butter #173430, heavy cream #170859) and are not sourced from a direct dish measurement; values carry moderate uncertainty. 3. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on cooked mushroom entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the literature. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from component analysis and not directly measured; these values are provided as best estimates and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis. 5. The dish is classified as Malaysian/Singaporean Zi Char cuisine; multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per the Singaporean cuisine multilingual requirement. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the vegetarian ingredient profile (no pork, no alcohol); formal halal certification status of the specific restaurant is unknown. 7. The input source (field survey data, Marina Seafood House – Boat Quay) is a delivery platform and is used only for food identity context, not as a nutritional source.
